name: Telecom Lobbying
description: Telecom lobbying in the United States refers to the efforts of telecommunications companies to influence government policy through direct contact with lawmakers and regulators, campaign contributions, and public relations campaigns. Proponents of conspiracy theories surrounding telecom lobbying often claim that this influence leads to policies that prioritize corporate profit over consumer interests, public safety, and technological innovation. These theories suggest that telecom companies actively suppress competition, control internet speeds and access, and resist regulations designed to protect privacy and net neutrality. They allege that this control is achieved through strategic donations to political campaigns, revolving door employment between government and industry, and the dissemination of misinformation. Mainstream perspectives acknowledge that telecom lobbying is a legitimate and significant part of the political process, but argue that it is subject to legal regulations and oversight. Scholars and journalists investigate the influence of lobbying on policy outcomes, but often attribute policy decisions to a complex interplay of factors, including public opinion, economic conditions, and partisan politics. Critics of conspiracy theories argue that they oversimplify the political process and exaggerate the power of telecom companies, ignoring the role of consumer advocacy groups, regulatory agencies, and competing interests within the industry. They also point to instances where regulations have been successfully implemented despite industry opposition, demonstrating the limitations of lobbying power.
subtitle: Influence and control in the digital age

claims.claim: Telecom companies suppress net neutrality through lobbying.
claims.evidence: Lobbying expenditures coinciding with the repeal of net neutrality rules.
claims.criticism: Repeal was also influenced by arguments about economic efficiency and innovation, with some arguing net neutrality stifled investment.

claims.claim: Telecom lobbying results in artificially high internet prices.
claims.evidence: Lack of competition in many markets, attributed to regulatory capture.
claims.criticism: High infrastructure costs and the need for continuous investment also contribute to pricing.

claims.claim: Telecom companies use lobbying to avoid data privacy regulations.
claims.evidence: Industry opposition to stronger privacy laws and data breach notification requirements.
claims.criticism: Industry argues compliance costs are too high and that existing regulations provide sufficient protection.

keyFigures.name: Ajit Pai
keyFigures.role: Former FCC Chairman who repealed net neutrality rules, seen as influenced by telecom lobbying.

keyFigures.name: Susan Crawford
keyFigures.role: Legal scholar and critic of telecom industry practices and lobbying efforts.

mythologyConnections: The idea of powerful, unseen forces controlling access to information and communication echoes themes of secret societies and hidden controllers found in various mythologies.

timeline.event: Telecommunications Act of 1996, deregulating the industry and increasing lobbying activities.
FCC adopts net neutrality rules under Title II of the Communications Act.

sources.author: Tim Wu
sources.work: The Master Switch: The Rise and Fall of Information Empires
sources.title: Tim Wu

sources.author: Lawrence Lessig
sources.work: Republic, Lost: How Money Corrupts Congress--and a Plan to Stop It
sources.title: Lawrence Lessig

sources.author: Thomas Hazlett
sources.work: The Political Spectrum: The Tumultuous Liberation of Wireless Technology from Government Control
